replies.author: Shovon Niverd
replies.content: It is interesting that they are saying AI lacks the capability of judging what should be the goal of a research or engineering problem. It looks like how Anthropic is progressing, very soon the human role in research will be to set the research questions.

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content: Indeed. The ability to ask questions and evaluate the responses from AI will become more important. Also, AI will have a profound impact on how research is done, just like how it impacts knowledge work in general.

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content: @channel Please join me to congratulate Shovon and Krishna, for their joint paper being accepted by IJCAI! IJCAI is a top-tier conference in AI, and their paper is titled "TabKD: Tabular Knowledge Distillation through Interaction Diversity of Learned Feature Bins". Shovon has done an excellent job on picking up the topic of Knowledge Distillation quickly from a pure Software Engineering background. I am also very happy to see a close, fruitful collaboration between Shovon and Krishna on this project. Well done!

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content:
@channel Recently I have noticed that a couple of you are using LLMs to write internal reports. For example, in a few cases, I see the phrase "The advisor's comment ...", which seems to refer to what I said/asked during our meetings. This is troubling as you likely did not even read the text from LLMs before you sent it to me. Worse, this defeats the purpose of writing the reports, which is to demonstrate your understand about the subject matter you are supposed to explore.

From now on, you are NOT allowed to use LLMs for writing internal reports, not even for polishing the text. Instead, you must write down your understanding in your own words. It is OK to use LLMs to help you understand concepts/papers, and to think with LLMs. But when it comes to write internal reports, you must write in your own words.

replies.author: Fadul Sikder
replies.content:
Professor Lei, I would like to respectfully push back on this idea a little. I believe that using AI to understand concepts and papers can be very important. I also think that using AI to help shape research ideas and communicate with the research group can have real value.

The first value is structure. Sometimes, when we try to write about our research, we face structural limitations, especially when we are trying to identify information gaps across different papers. For example, if I want to write a detailed report that looks at context across several papers and synthesizes them into one note in my own words, it can be difficult to maintain concentration and complete the task within a reasonable time frame. In this sense, AI can help provide structure for research communication and help organize the information we want to communicate about our research projects.

The second point is language. Many students think through ideas in their native language first. When they try to develop research ideas, they may naturally explain those ideas in their native language and then translate them into English. However, there is often a difference between making a research argument in one’s native language and formulating that same argument clearly in English, especially when English is a second language. From this perspective, AI can help translate and clarify a person’s ideas and arguments so they can be communicated more effectively in research discussions.

My third point is that AI use could be allowed in a limited and transparent way. Everyone should include a disclaimer explaining how and to what extent AI was used. There could also be a specific section in each report where the writer clearly states their own response, understanding, or thoughts about the research arguments or communications being discussed. This section could be labeled “My Own Response,” “My Own Understanding,” or “My Own Thoughts,” so it is clear which parts reflect the student’s personal understanding and argument.

The fourth point I want to emphasize is that the real issue is whether people understand what they are reporting. Even if they do not have a complete understanding, they should at least demonstrate some level of understanding. Any response, whether produced with AI or without AI, should go through a synthesis process so that it becomes the writer’s own. The goal should be to move toward a state of understanding that allows us to produce good research outcomes. So I think the main question everyone should honestly answer is this: when they report something using an LLM, do they actually understand what they are trying to communicate, or is it simply an LLM-generated response produced for the sake of having a response?

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content:
Well, I don't really buy your arguments. Let me respond to your points one by one: The first point, structure, is about thinking. You can definitely think with LLMs on the structure. For the second point, language, I don't really care whether your use of English is polished in an internal report. I care much more about whether you truly understand what you write. The third point applies only if the use of AI is necessary. Also, I only care about your section labeled "My Own Response/Understanding/Thoughts", whereas you can think with LLMs to come up with this section. The forth point does not conflict with the new policy.

What I do agree is that the fundamental issue is about whether people understand what they are reporting. But one common problem is that you do not know whether you truly understand. Writing in your own words is one way to help you check whether you truly understand, and further the process of writing can help you further your understanding. This is like in a class, you are allowed to discuss with other people or use whatever resources you can get hold of to develop your own understanding, but you must write down your understanding in your own words.

I welcome other inputs to this new policy. But you would have to come up with a good argument first. Keep this in mind as you consider this policy. What do you really want to accomplish when you write an internal report, and for the matter, when you do research?

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content: Another point related to this: I noticed some of you start to write very long internal reports. While I appreciate your effort, this is not really necessary, and it is certainly not the best way to use your time. I don't look for polished reports. Instead, I look for reports that are concise but make meaningful points. In several cases, the reports are written based on inaccurate/incorrect understandings. The time you spend on trying to get a very polished report can be spent on developing actual ideas. Also, this seems to add significant delays to your responses. Instead of long reports, I would rather have more timely exchanges on interesting ideas and real points.

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content:
A few important comments about presentations (based on feedback from Fadul's proposal):


1. It is critical to make your presentation from the committee/audience perspective. It is not about what you want to say. Instead, it is about what the audience is looking for.
2. Considering the time limit (typically no more than 1 hour), you must focus on the big picture and main ideas in a way that people who are not familiar with your specific projects can understand.
3. Before a milestone meeting, you must send to the committee the slides and the necessary documents in good shape in advance. You should try your best to send them one week in advance, if at all possible. 

I want to emphasize these points as otherwise, you are at significant risk for failing the presentation/milestone.
